notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s how all of this fits together for 2026 if you have W‑2 income from Microsoft, contribute to a 401(k), and have some freelance income. This is general education, not tax advice.

If you want this tailored (like “given my income of $X, what actually applies?”), I’d need:

Your approximate 2026 W‑2 income from Microsoft
Your expected 2026 freelance profit (income minus expenses)
Your age in 2026
Confirmation you’re covered by a workplace plan (you are, via the Microsoft 401(k))
1. Microsoft 401(k) and possible “mega backdoor” in 2026

For 2026, the 401(k) limits are:

Item | 2026 Amount | Notes
---|---|---
Employee elective deferral | $24,500 | Traditional or Roth 401(k), combined
Catch-up (age 50–59, 64+) | $8,000 | On top of $24,500
Super catch-up (age 60–63) | $11,250 | On top of $24,500
Total employee deferral age 50–59 or 64+ | $32,500 | 24,500 + 8,000
Total employee deferral age 60–63 | $35,750 | 24,500 + 11,250
Overall 415(c) limit (all contributions) | $72,000 | Employee + employer + after-tax

Key points for you:

Your $24,500 limit is shared between traditional and Roth 401(k) at Microsoft.
The employer match doesn’t count toward the $24,500, but it does count toward the $72,000 overall cap.
If the plan allows after-tax contributions and in‑plan Roth conversions or rollouts to a Roth IRA, that’s the mega backdoor Roth:
Room for after-tax = $72,000 − (your deferrals + employer match + any profit sharing).
Pitfall: Not every Microsoft plan setup allows this; you’d need to check the benefits site/plan document.
Another pitfall: Going over $24,500 in pre‑tax/Roth deferrals (instead of after‑tax) creates excess deferrals that have to be fixed.

Big takeaway:
$24,500 is your personal deferral cap; $72,000 is the all‑in cap (you + Microsoft + after‑tax).

2. IRA / Roth IRA eligibility and 2026 limits

For 2026, typical IRA limits (pattern-based, final numbers will come from the IRS):

IRA contribution limit: likely around $7,000 (under 50) and $8,000+ (50+).
That limit is combined across traditional and Roth IRAs.

Rules to keep in mind:

You can contribute up to the lesser of:
The annual limit, or
Your earned income (W‑2 + net self‑employment).
Being covered by the Microsoft 401(k) affects:
Whether a traditional IRA contribution is deductible (phased out by MAGI).
Roth IRA contributions also phase out with MAGI:
Single: phaseout band in 2026 will likely be in the low‑ to mid‑$100Ks (exact numbers pending).
Married filing jointly: higher band, historically roughly double single.

Common gotchas:

Mixing up “can I contribute?” with “can I deduct it?”
You can often still contribute to a traditional IRA even if the deduction phases out.
Going over the Roth IRA income limits:
If Microsoft + freelance income pushes you too high, direct Roth contributions may be partially or fully disallowed.
Some people use a backdoor Roth IRA (nondeductible traditional IRA → Roth conversion), but the pro‑rata rule kicks in if you have other pre‑tax IRA balances.

Big takeaway:
Eligibility, deductibility, and Roth income limits are three separate levers.

3. Self-employment tax & estimates on freelance income

Your freelance profit is:

Subject to income tax (federal and maybe state/city).
Subject to self‑employment (SE) tax if net profit is ≥ $400:
SE tax is about 15.3% on net earnings up to the Social Security wage base
12.4% Social Security + 2.9% Medicare
Plus 0.9% additional Medicare above certain income thresholds.

Key details:

Net earnings = freelance income − ordinary and necessary business expenses.
You can deduct ½ of your SE tax as an above‑the‑line adjustment to income.
If you expect to owe $1,000+ for 2026 after withholding and credits, the IRS generally wants quarterly estimated payments (Form 1040‑ES).
With W‑2 income, you can either:
Increase withholding at Microsoft (new W‑4) to cover freelance taxes, or
Make separate quarterly estimates.

Common pitfalls:

Treating freelance money as “extra” and not reserving anything for SE tax.
Skipping quarterly estimates and getting underpayment penalties, even if you pay in full at filing.
Forgetting SE tax is on top of regular income tax.

Big takeaway:
Freelance profit usually needs its own tax set‑aside plan, even if your W‑2 withholding feels “fine.”

4. Business deductions for your freelance work

For your freelance work (usually Schedule C), typical deductible categories if they’re ordinary and necessary:

Home office (used regularly and exclusively for business).
Equipment and software (laptop, tools, subscriptions).
Internet and phone (business portion).
Professional services (accounting, legal, design help).
Education tied to your freelance skills.
Travel and meals (with strict documentation; meals usually 50% deductible).
Health insurance premiums (if self‑employed and not covered elsewhere; special rules apply).

Pitfalls:

Mixing personal and business spending without clean records.
Overstating home office or vehicle use without backup.
Not tracking mileage or actual vehicle costs when you use a car for business.

Big takeaway:
Good records turn freelance income into lower taxable profit; sloppy records do the opposite.

5. Other 2026 tax planning moves for W‑2 + freelance

For your mix of Microsoft W‑2 and freelance:

Max out tax‑advantaged space (conceptually)

Microsoft 401(k) up to $24,500 (plus catch‑ups if you’re eligible).
Consider IRA or Roth IRA contributions if you qualify.
If freelance income is meaningful and ongoing, some people look at a solo 401(k) or SEP‑IRA for that income, but:
Employee deferral limits are shared across all employers, so this gets more complex when you already have a big employer plan.

Health accounts

If you’re on a high‑deductible health plan, an HSA can be very tax‑efficient (triple tax advantage).
2026 HSA limits will be set by the IRS and are indexed each year.

Charitable giving

If you itemize, charitable contributions can be deductible.
Some people use donor‑advised funds in high‑income years (like big RSU vesting) to bunch deductions.

Stock compensation (very common at Microsoft)

RSUs and ESPP have their own tax timing and withholding rules.
Under‑withholding on RSUs is a very common reason for surprise tax bills.

If you’d like to narrow this down to what actually matters for you, share:

Your age in 2026
A rough 2026 W‑2 income range (for example, $100–150K, $150–250K, etc.)
A rough 2026 freelance profit estimate

Then Monarch can walk through which limits, phaseouts, and strategies are most relevant to your situation, still in an educational (not advice) way.
